params.text:
Why accurate records matter

Good record keeping sits at the heart of safe, joined-up care. Every note you write is a message to the next person who supports the individual — the colleague on the next shift, the district nurse, the GP, or an inspector. When records are clear and complete, care stays consistent and safe even when the people delivering it change.

  • Continuity and safety of care: the next worker can see exactly what happened, what was given, and what to watch for.
  • Communication: records pass information reliably between staff, shifts and other services, so nothing is missed or repeated.
  • Legal and professional evidence: your records may be reviewed by the Care Quality Commission, a coroner, a safeguarding enquiry or a court.
  • Accountability: a signed, dated entry shows who did what, and when.

There is a well-known saying in health and social care: “If it isn't recorded, it didn't happen.” If care was given but never written down, there is no proof it took place — and no way for others to build on it safely.

params.question: In care, the phrase "if it isn't recorded, it didn't happen" means that undocumented care cannot be evidenced and is treated as though it never took place.

l10n.wrongAnswerMessage: Not quite. The saying is a reminder that unrecorded care leaves no evidence — for the person, for colleagues, or for an inspector.
l10n.correctAnswerMessage: Correct. However good the care was, without a record there is no proof it happened and colleagues cannot rely on it.
